Rule stating that distributors, importers, or deployers become providers if they put their name on a system, make substantial modifications, or change the intended purpose of an AI system to become high-risk.

**REGULATION (EC) NO 765/2008** serves as a foundational component of the European Union’s New Legislative Framework. It is the primary regulation that establishes the general principles and requirements for CE marking, which signifies that a product conforms to EU health, safety, and environmental protection standards. Furthermore, REGULATION (EC) NO 765/2008 provides essential rules to ensure consistency regarding the placing of products on the market, including those categorized as high-risk AI systems.
Methodology developed by the AI Office to guide evaluations and reviews regarding risk levels and classification inclusion
Administrative fines for non-compliance with prohibited AI practices under Article 5. Amount: up to EUR 35,000,000 or 7% of total worldwide annual turnover, whichever is higher

**REGULATION (EU) 2022/2065**, formally known as the **Regulation on a Single Market for Digital Services** and commonly referred to as the **Digital Services Act (DSA)**, serves as a pivotal piece of legislation governing the digital ecosystem within the European Union.

This regulation establishes a comprehensive framework for online platforms and search engines, focusing on their responsibilities regarding content moderation and the mitigation of systemic risks. Specifically, it provides critical guidelines for how these entities must handle notices concerning illegal content and address broader safety concerns online.

The scope of **REGULATION (EU) 2022/2065** encompasses extensive obligations for hosting services and online platforms. It plays a foundational role in shaping digital accountability, and its provisions are distinct from, and remain unaffected by, other legislative efforts such as the AI Act. This separation is highlighted in Recital 136 of the AI Act, which references the DSA to clarify that the specific liability provisions and regulatory obligations concerning content moderation and systemic risk mitigation established under the DSA continue to apply independently of AI-specific regulations. Consequently, **REGULATION (EU) 2022/2065** functions as a cornerstone for ensuring a safer and more transparent digital environment across the European Single Market.

A **DATA SUBJECT** is defined as a natural person whose personal data is processed, including instances where this processing is carried out by AI systems. Regardless of the context in which their data is handled, the **DATA SUBJECT** retains the full scope of rights and guarantees afforded to them under Union data protection law, such as the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and the Law Enforcement Directive (LED). These protections include, but are not limited to, the right to withdraw consent for the processing of their personal data. Consequently, the **DATA SUBJECT** remains a protected party within the framework of data governance, ensuring their rights are upheld even when their information is utilized by emerging technologies like artificial intelligence.

Market Surveillance Authorities are national bodies tasked with the responsibility of overseeing and enforcing market regulations. These authorities play a critical role in cross-border market surveillance, a process in which they receive essential support from a dedicated scientific panel to enhance the effectiveness of their operations.

Furthermore, Market Surveillance Authorities are empowered with specific access rights to information that is registered within the European Union database, as established under Article 60. To ensure consistency and cooperation across different jurisdictions, these national authorities actively collaborate within the standing sub-groups of the Board, in accordance with the provisions outlined in Article 65(6). Through this combination of data access, scientific support, and structured inter-authority cooperation, Market Surveillance Authorities maintain regulatory oversight and integrity across markets.

The Union Safeguard Procedure is a formal regulatory mechanism defined under Article 81 of the relevant legislation. Within this framework, the European Commission is tasked with evaluating specific national measures implemented by market surveillance authorities in response to concerns regarding AI systems. This procedure serves as a structured oversight process, ensuring that actions taken against AI systems at a national level are subject to central review and assessment by the Commission.

Condition under which a biometric categorisation system is excluded from regulation if the feature is purely ancillary, intrinsically linked to a principal commercial service, and not a means of circumventing the Regulation.

A Written Agreement is a mandatory formal contract established between a provider and a third party within the AI value chain. This agreement functions as a critical requirement, ensuring that all relevant parties involved in the AI value chain are obligated to furnish the provider with necessary technical assistance and information access. By formalizing these obligations, the Written Agreement ensures seamless cooperation and the provision of essential resources required for the effective functioning and development of AI systems.
Obligation for law enforcement to comply with Article 10 of Directive (EU) 2016/680 when using AI systems for biometric identification (Recital 94)

Biometric categorisation systems are AI-powered technologies that process the biometric data of natural persons to classify them based on specific characteristics. Due to the potential risks these systems pose to fundamental rights and privacy, they are subject to strict regulatory oversight.

Broadly, these systems are defined by their capacity to categorize individuals based on biometric data, and they face specific restrictions, particularly within the context of police and judicial cooperation. When used in these law enforcement and judicial settings, the deployment of such systems is tightly controlled to ensure accountability and adherence to legal standards.

Furthermore, biometric categorisation systems are subject to outright prohibition when they are designed or used to infer sensitive attributes about individuals. This includes the use of AI to derive information regarding a person’s political opinions, religious or philosophical beliefs, racial or ethnic origin, or sexual orientation. Because the inference of such protected characteristics carries a high risk of discrimination and abuse, the use of biometric categorisation systems for these purposes is forbidden to ensure the protection of personal data and individual privacy.

Article 42 addresses the presumption of conformity for high-risk AI systems. Specifically, it establishes provisions under which these systems are presumed to satisfy certain requirements based on their adherence to specific standards. This presumption is granted when high-risk AI systems demonstrate compliance through either the quality of their training data or by obtaining relevant cybersecurity certification. Consequently, Article 42 serves as a mechanism to streamline regulatory compliance for high-risk AI systems by validating their safety and reliability through these established benchmarks.

**ARTICLE 14** serves as the primary legal provision governing the mandate for human oversight of high-risk AI systems. It establishes the critical framework for ensuring that such technologies remain subject to meaningful human control, thereby defining both the overarching requirements and the specific implementation measures necessary for compliance.

As the regulation governing human oversight, **ARTICLE 14** mandates that high-risk AI systems must be designed and developed in such a way that they can be effectively overseen by human agents. This oversight is intended to prevent or minimize risks that could arise during the system's operation. By detailing these requirements, **ARTICLE 14** provides the necessary legal context for specific oversight actions, ensuring that human intervention is not merely a formality but a functional component of the AI system's lifecycle. Consequently, it acts as a foundational element for maintaining accountability, safety, and human agency in the deployment of high-risk artificial intelligence.

**ANNEX IV**

ANNEX IV serves as a critical regulatory component, defining the mandatory requirements for the technical documentation associated with AI systems. It functions as a foundational reference document that specifies the minimum elements that must be included in this documentation to ensure compliance and transparency.

The scope of ANNEX IV is referenced across various provisions of the regulatory framework. Specifically, it is explicitly cited in Article 11(1) regarding general technical documentation obligations and in Article 23(1)(b). Furthermore, the requirements outlined in ANNEX IV are designed to be accessible to market surveillance authorities, ensuring they have the necessary information to perform their regulatory oversight functions effectively.

Among the specific elements mandated by ANNEX IV is the inclusion of a post-market monitoring plan, which is essential for ongoing safety and performance assessment. Because the technological landscape for AI is constantly evolving, ANNEX IV is subject to potential updates through delegated acts, allowing the requirements to remain relevant and effective over time. In summary, ANNEX IV establishes the essential standards for technical documentation, ensuring that AI systems are properly documented, monitored, and accountable to oversight bodies.
Practices covering design choices, data collection, preparation, and bias mitigation required for training, validation, and testing data sets for high-risk AI systems per Article 10

**Robustness**, in the context of artificial intelligence, refers to a critical technical requirement for AI systems to maintain stable, reliable, and consistent performance under varying conditions, adverse circumstances, and unforeseen situations. Specifically, for high-risk AI systems, robustness is a fundamental prerequisite for regulatory compliance.

This characteristic demands that systems remain resilient against errors, faults, inconsistencies, and harmful or undesirable behaviors. To achieve this level of reliability, robustness is supported by a combination of both technical and organizational measures. These strategies often involve the implementation of redundancy and the creation of fail-safe plans, ensuring that the system can withstand potential disruptions or unexpected inputs without compromising its operational integrity. As referenced in regulatory frameworks such as Recital 77, robustness ensures that AI systems function predictably and safely, even when faced with challenging environments.

A Competent Public Authority is defined as any public authority, including relevant Union institutions, bodies, or agencies, that is mandated to perform essential duties related to migration, asylum, and border control management. These entities hold responsibilities that extend to administrative and law enforcement enforcement tasks within these domains. Furthermore, in the context of technological oversight and governance, the term specifically encompasses those authorities that are actively involved in the deployment of artificial intelligence (AI) systems to facilitate or execute their mandated operations in migration, asylum, border control, or administrative enforcement.

The "INVESTIGATION OBLIGATION" represents a multifaceted requirement ensuring accountability and oversight within regulatory frameworks. This obligation mandates distinct duties for different stakeholders involved in compliance and safety management.

Primarily, the "INVESTIGATION OBLIGATION" imposes a duty on the provider to proactively manage safety. Following a serious incident, the provider is required to conduct thorough investigations, perform necessary risk assessments, and implement appropriate corrective actions to mitigate future hazards and ensure ongoing safety.

Simultaneously, the "INVESTIGATION OBLIGATION" extends to regulatory oversight bodies. It requires the notifying authority to investigate with diligence whenever there is reasonable cause to believe that a notified body is failing to meet its established requirements or professional obligations. This dual layer of responsibility ensures that both the service providers and the bodies responsible for their assessment are held accountable for maintaining rigorous standards, thereby upholding system integrity and public safety.

An innovative AI system refers to an artificial intelligence technology that is undergoing development, training, testing, or validation within the structured environment of an AI regulatory sandbox. These systems are specifically designed to be cultivated within this controlled space prior to their formal market entry. The primary purpose of utilizing an AI regulatory sandbox for these systems is to ensure that they are fully compliant with the relevant Regulation and all other applicable laws and standards before they are released to the public or placed on the market. By operating within this framework, developers can thoroughly test and validate the AI system's functionality and adherence to legal requirements, thereby mitigating risks and ensuring responsible deployment.

The Single Information Platform is a digital tool developed and maintained by the AI Office to serve as a comprehensive resource for operators, providers, and deployers under the AI Act. Its primary purpose is to offer accessible, easy-to-use information regarding the Regulation.

Additionally, the Single Information Platform functions as a mechanism for transparency regarding sandbox projects. Under the provisions of the Article, it serves as a designated space where exit reports from these projects may be made publicly available, provided that there is explicit agreement between the provider and the relevant competent authority. By combining informational resources with this specific reporting function, the platform supports compliance and clarity for stakeholders navigating the AI Act.
Specific requirement to label AI-generated or manipulated output; limited by exceptions for assistive functions, standard editing, or human-reviewed content
Mandatory methods such as watermarks, metadata identifications, cryptographic methods, or logging methods to identify AI-generated or manipulated content.

Social scoring AI systems are technologies designed to evaluate, classify, or rank natural persons or groups based on their social behavior, personality traits, or personal characteristics over a period of time. Because these systems often result in discriminatory or detrimental outcomes for the individuals or groups being assessed, they are subject to strict regulatory prohibition. Specifically, as outlined in Recital 31, these systems are banned due to the risks they pose in unfairly categorizing and negatively impacting individuals based on their inherent characteristics or behavioral patterns.
Specific obligation for certain high-risk AI systems (law enforcement, migration, etc.) to be registered in a secure, non-public section of the EU database rather than the public section

The Prohibition of AI Practices encompasses a critical regulatory framework designed to safeguard individuals and society from harmful artificial intelligence technologies. At its core, this framework establishes an overarching requirement to ban specific AI systems deemed to violate human rights, undermine dignity, or pose excessive risks to fundamental freedoms, as detailed in Recitals 30, 31, and 33. To enforce these protections, Article 5 explicitly outlines a set of specific prohibitions regarding AI practices that all operators are legally mandated to comply with. Together, these measures create a protective boundary, ensuring that the deployment of AI remains consistent with ethical standards and legal obligations, prioritizing the safety and rights of the public over the unchecked implementation of high-risk AI technologies.

Narrowly defined situations allowing the use of real-time remote biometric identification, including search for crime victims, threats to life/safety/terrorist attacks, and localisation of suspects of serious criminal offences (Recital 33).
AI systems used for prevention, detection, investigation, and prosecution of criminal offences, excluding specific administrative tasks in tax/customs/financial intelligence units.
Prohibition on the placing on the market, putting into service, or use of AI systems intended to detect emotional states in workplace and education contexts (Recital 44)

Article 72 serves as a critical regulatory provision within the legal framework governing artificial intelligence, specifically focusing on the post-market monitoring of AI systems. Its primary objective is to establish comprehensive requirements for the post-market monitoring systems that providers of high-risk AI systems must implement.

By formalizing these requirements, Article 72 functions as the legal basis for ensuring ongoing oversight and accountability after an AI system has been deployed. It mandates that providers establish a systematic procedure to actively collect, document, and analyze experience gained from the use of their high-risk AI systems. This provision is explicitly linked to the broader regulatory structure, specifically referenced in Article 9(2)(c), which highlights its role in the integrated compliance strategy for AI.

Furthermore, Article 72 delineates clear reporting obligations for providers and other associated actors. These obligations are designed to ensure that relevant information concerning the performance, safety, and potential issues of AI systems is effectively communicated and acted upon. This includes regulating the necessary communication between deployers and providers, facilitating a flow of information that allows for timely updates, risk mitigation, and improvements to the AI systems in response to real-world usage.

In summary, Article 72 is the definitive provision mandating the post-market monitoring of AI systems. It creates the regulatory architecture through which providers must oversee their high-risk AI tools, ensuring that they maintain safety and performance standards throughout the system's operational lifecycle through rigorous monitoring and transparent reporting mechanisms.

Delegated acts modifying conditions refer to regulatory measures adopted by the European Commission designed to amend specific criteria related to the high-risk classification of AI systems. These acts provide the Commission with the authority to update and refine the regulatory framework established under Article 49 of the relevant AI legislation.

Specifically, these delegated acts serve two primary functions: first, they allow the Commission to amend the conditions outlined in Article 49(3) regarding the general criteria for the high-risk classification of AI systems. Second, they empower the Commission to modify the conditions and criteria established under Article 49(6) and (7), which pertain to the specific processes for excluding certain AI systems from being categorized as high-risk. By providing a mechanism to adjust these parameters, the delegated acts ensure that the classification framework remains adaptable to technological advancements and evolving safety considerations.

**AFFECTED PERSONS** are defined as individuals or entities whose interests, rights, and freedoms are impacted by artificial intelligence systems. This group comprises natural or legal persons whose rights and freedoms are adversely impacted by the use of AI systems, as well as those who are directly affected by decisions made by a deployer based on the output of high-risk AI. Furthermore, AFFECTED PERSONS include those whose specific interests and needs must be taken into account by the AI Office and the Board when they are establishing the objectives for codes of practice.

**Safety Component AI Systems**

A Safety Component AI System is defined as an artificial intelligence system that functions as a safety component of a product, or serves as a product in its own right. These systems are specifically characterized by their role in ensuring the safe operation of the technology they are integrated into or constitute. Due to the critical nature of their function, these AI systems are subject to Union harmonization legislation. When an AI system acts as a safety component, it must be evaluated for its potential to cause adverse impacts on human health and safety; consequently, such systems are often classified as high-risk under applicable regulatory frameworks, as outlined in provisions such as Recital 47.

Regarding the log keeping requirements for high-risk AI systems, both providers and deployers have specific responsibilities to ensure accountability and traceability. 

Providers are mandated to retain automatically generated logs created by their high-risk AI systems, provided these systems remain under their control. This requirement is explicitly outlined under Article 16(e) and Article 19 of the relevant regulations. Furthermore, deployers are also required to maintain logs generated by these high-risk AI systems during their operation.

In both instances, the retention period for these logs is a minimum of six months. This duration applies universally unless a different timeframe is specified or mandated by other applicable Union or national laws. By adhering to these log-keeping practices, both providers and deployers help ensure the transparency and regulatory compliance of high-risk AI systems.

The "INFORMATION OBLIGATION" encompasses specific transparency requirements aimed at both providers and deployers of regulated systems to ensure clarity and accountability.

For providers, the obligation mandates the clear disclosure of their identity. Specifically, providers are required to prominently indicate their name, their registered trade name or trademark, and a valid contact address. This information must be made available directly on the system itself, on its packaging, or within the accompanying documentation, as stipulated by Article 16(b). 

Furthermore, the "INFORMATION OBLIGATION" extends to the operational usage of sensitive technologies. Deployers of AI systems are required to inform natural persons whenever they are being exposed to emotion recognition or biometric categorization systems. This ensures that individuals are aware of the deployment of such invasive technologies in their environment, upholding transparency standards regarding the use of these systems. 

In summary, the "INFORMATION OBLIGATION" functions to bridge the gap between technology providers, deployers, and the public by ensuring that the origin of a system is verifiable and that the application of high-impact AI tools is communicated to those affected by them.

**ESSENTIAL PUBLIC SERVICES AI SYSTEM**

The ESSENTIAL PUBLIC SERVICES AI SYSTEM refers to artificial intelligence technologies utilized by public authorities to make critical determinations regarding individual eligibility for vital services and support. These systems play a central role in managing access to essential resources, including healthcare, social security, and housing assistance. Due to the significant impact these decisions have on the lives and well-being of citizens, such AI systems are officially classified as high-risk, necessitating rigorous oversight and ethical standards to ensure fairness, accountability, and accuracy in their administrative functions.

Article 96 serves as the governing provision regarding the development and implementation of guidelines by the Commission. Specifically, it establishes the legal framework that authorizes the Commission to create guidelines necessary for the proper implementation of the Regulation. Furthermore, Article 96 provides the procedural basis for the implementation of specific guidance concerning Article 6, acting as the primary reference article for these related regulatory requirements.

Article 49 serves as a key regulatory provision concerning the administrative management of artificial intelligence technologies within the European Union. Specifically, it establishes and outlines the registration obligations for high-risk AI systems. This article functions as the primary legal mandate governing the mandatory registration of these systems in the centralized EU database, thereby defining the specific requirements that must be met to ensure compliance and transparency for high-risk AI deployments.

The European Union Agency for Cybersecurity, commonly referred to as ENISA, serves as the central authority responsible for promoting and ensuring a high level of cybersecurity throughout the European Union. Established and regulated by Regulation (EU) 2019/881—also known as the Cybersecurity Act—ENISA functions as the primary body tasked with developing and managing cybersecurity certification frameworks.

In its capacity as the EU's dedicated cybersecurity agency, ENISA provides vital knowledge and expertise to support the formulation and implementation of cybersecurity policies. This includes offering guidance on technical tasks and strategic initiatives, as referenced in Recital 79 of the relevant legislation. Furthermore, the agency plays a crucial role in the evolving technological landscape by cooperating directly with the European Commission on matters concerning the security of artificial intelligence (AI) systems. Through these diverse responsibilities, ENISA acts as a cornerstone for maintaining digital resilience and security across the Union.
A duty of the European Commission to assess the enforcement of the Regulation by 2 August 2031 and report findings.

entity_name: MIGRATION, ASYLUM AND BORDER CONTROL MANAGEMENT
description: Area involving AI systems used for border management, including polygraphs, risk assessment of entrants, and processing of asylum/visa applications, as listed in Annex III(7)

**REGISTRATION OF TESTING**

Under Article 60 of the relevant regulations, the "REGISTRATION OF TESTING" framework mandates that entities conducting testing for AI systems in real-world conditions must fulfill a specific duty to register and disclose comprehensive information. This registration process is required to ensure transparency and oversight during real-world trials.

To comply with this mandate, the entity must provide five key categories of information:

1.  **Identification:** Unique identification details for the testing operation.
2.  **Contact Information:** Specific contact details for the providers and/or deployers responsible for the testing.
3.  **System Description:** A clear and detailed description of the AI system being subjected to the real-world testing.
4.  **Test Plan Summary:** A concise summary outlining the overarching plan and objectives of the test.
5.  **Status Information:** Current information regarding the suspension or termination status of the testing activity.

By consolidating these requirements, the "REGISTRATION OF TESTING" ensures that regulators and relevant authorities maintain a complete record of AI systems being tested in uncontrolled environments, thereby facilitating better monitoring and accountability for the deployment of these technologies.

The High-Level Expert Group on Artificial Intelligence, commonly referred to as the AI HLEG, is an independent body appointed by the European Commission. Established to provide expert guidance on AI development and deployment, the AI HLEG is primarily recognized for its pivotal role in formulating ethical standards for the technology.

Most notably, the AI HLEG was responsible for developing the 2019 Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI. This foundational document was authored by the group to establish a clear framework for ensuring that artificial intelligence systems are developed and utilized in a manner that is both ethical and trustworthy. These guidelines have subsequently served as a critical resource, helping to inform and shape the broader regulatory framework for AI within the European Union. By providing this guidance, the AI HLEG has played a significant role in navigating the complex intersection of technological innovation, ethical responsibility, and governance.

**ANNEX X** refers to a specific section within the EU AI Act that serves to identify and list relevant Union legislative acts. This annex focuses exclusively on legislation pertaining to large-scale information technology (IT) systems within the domain of Freedom, Security, and Justice. The information contained in ANNEX X is particularly significant because it is often utilized to help determine the classification of AI systems, specifically when evaluating whether a system qualifies as a high-risk AI system under the regulations established by the AI Act.

The Schengen Information System (SIS) is a large-scale IT system dedicated to the management of security and borders. It is formally recognized as a relevant Union legislative act, specifically listed under Annex X of the applicable regulations governing its operation.

The Visa Information System is a large-scale information technology system utilized for the management of visa applications and visa processing. As a critical component of the relevant Union legislative framework, it is explicitly listed in Annex X. Currently, the Visa Information System is subject to ongoing efforts to reform its legal framework, which are also detailed within the regulations listed in Annex X.

Article 16 of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union (TFEU) serves as a fundamental legal basis within the European Union's regulatory framework, particularly concerning the governance of Artificial Intelligence. It provides the essential treaty-based authorization for the rules established under the AI Act, which govern the processing of personal data and dictate the limitations on specific technologies. 

Specifically, Article 16 TFEU provides the necessary authority to enact legislation concerning the protection of personal data by AI systems. This includes the implementation of strict regulations regarding how personal data is handled, especially within sensitive fields such as law enforcement, where data processing requires heightened oversight. Furthermore, this provision acts as the legal foundation for the AI Act’s prohibitions on certain biometric uses, ensuring that the deployment of such technologies remains consistent with EU data protection principles and fundamental rights.
A formal certificate issued by a notified body to a provider confirming that an AI system conforms with the requirements set out in Chapter III, Section 2

entity_name: AI SYSTEM FOR LAW ENFORCEMENT VICTIM ASSESSMENT
description: System used to assess the risk of a natural person becoming the victim of criminal offences, identified in Annex III(6)(a)
AI systems used by law enforcement for assessing criminal victimisation risk, as polygraphs, or for evaluation purposes.

Regulation (EU) No 575/2013 is a piece of legislation that establishes prudential requirements for credit institutions and investment firms. This regulation is explicitly referenced in Recital 46 of relevant financial services legislation, particularly in the context of the scope of artificial intelligence (AI) applications within the financial sector. By setting these foundational prudential standards, the regulation plays a critical role in framing the operational and regulatory environment for financial entities as they integrate new technologies.

Regulation (EU) 2019/881, widely known as the Cybersecurity Act, is a pivotal piece of European Union legislation that addresses the safety and resilience of digital infrastructure. Its primary function is to establish a comprehensive framework for the cybersecurity certification of information and communications technology (ICT) products, services, and processes.

Central to this regulation is the formal establishment and reinforcement of ENISA, the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity. Regulation (EU) 2019/881 defines the agency’s mandate, outlines its specific tasks, and formalizes the expertise required for ENISA to fulfill its role as the EU’s central cybersecurity authority. By setting these parameters, the regulation ensures that ENISA is equipped to effectively support member states in identifying and mitigating cybersecurity threats.

Beyond its role regarding ENISA, the Cybersecurity Act creates a unified certification framework across the EU. This framework is designed to build trust and consistency in the digital single market by enabling the development of certification schemes for various ICT solutions. This certification infrastructure is particularly significant in the context of emerging technologies; for instance, the regulation is noted for its relevance to the presumption of conformity for high-risk artificial intelligence (AI) systems.

The regulation is frequently cited throughout broader EU legal documentation. For example, it is specifically referenced in Recital 37, which highlights its function as a foundational framework for ICT cybersecurity certification. Additionally, it is referenced in Recital 78, which emphasizes the regulation's role in establishing the mandate and operational scope of ENISA. In summary, Regulation (EU) 2019/881 serves as the cornerstone for EU cybersecurity governance, bridging the gap between agency oversight and practical security certification for digital products.

A conformity assessment procedure based on internal checks. For high-risk AI systems that are also important and critical products with digital elements, this procedure is subject to specific derogations defined in Recital 78.

entity_name: REGULATION ON HORIZONTAL CYBERSECURITY REQUIREMENTS FOR PRODUCTS WITH DIGITAL ELEMENTS
description: A legislative framework that defines essential cybersecurity requirements for products with digital elements, which high-risk AI systems may leverage for compliance.

Criteria used by the Commission to designate general-purpose AI models with systemic risk. Includes: number of parameters, dataset quality/size, computation amount (FLOPs/cost/time/energy), input/output modalities, and model benchmarks/evaluations, as specified in Annex XIII.

**PERSONAL DATA PROTECTION** is a mandatory regulatory standard that safeguards the privacy and rights of natural persons concerning the processing of their personal information. This framework is anchored in legal requirements, most notably the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) (Regulation 2016/679), which establishes the protocols for how personal data must be handled, stored, and managed. 

Furthermore, PERSONAL DATA PROTECTION extends to modern technological landscapes, serving as a critical standard with which AI systems must comply. As automated technologies and artificial intelligence increasingly process vast quantities of information, they are strictly required to adhere to these data protection principles to ensure that the processing of personal data remains lawful, transparent, and secure, thereby preventing the infringement of individual privacy rights.

Fundamental Rights, as enshrined in the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union, serve as a critical benchmark for the regulation and governance of Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems. The primary purpose of AI-related regulation is to ensure that these technologies do not infringe upon, but rather protect, the essential liberties and protections guaranteed to individuals under the Charter.

When assessing the potential adverse impacts of AI systems, it is mandatory to consider a comprehensive range of these fundamental rights. Central to this evaluation are core principles such as human dignity, the right to non-discrimination, the right to privacy, and the specific protections afforded to children’s rights. AI systems must be designed and deployed in a manner that upholds, rather than compromises, these foundational values.

Furthermore, the scope of Fundamental Rights within the context of AI extends to legal and procedural protections. It is essential that AI systems do not hamper or undermine fundamental rights related to the judicial process, including the right to an effective remedy, the right to a fair trial, the rights of the defense, and the presumption of innocence. By integrating these considerations into the development and oversight of AI, the regulatory framework aims to prevent harmful effects and ensure that technological advancement aligns with the legal and ethical standards established by the European Union.
Criteria for classifying an AI system as high-risk, including being a safety component of products subject to third-party assessment or operating in pre-defined high-risk areas

The EU Database Registration process is a critical regulatory obligation for providers of AI systems. According to the governing regulations, providers are required to register their AI systems in the designated EU database before the systems are deployed for use.

While Article 49 mandates this registration prior to use, there is a specific exception for urgent situations. In such cases, an AI system may be utilized before the registration process is finalized, provided that the registration is completed immediately thereafter without undue delay.

Furthermore, it is a specific requirement to register high-risk AI systems in the EU database, as outlined in Article 71. Compliance with these registration mandates is mandatory; a failure to register the AI system correctly constitutes formal non-compliance under Article 83, which may carry regulatory consequences. Therefore, ensuring timely and accurate registration is essential for legal operation within the European Union.

Temporary testing of an AI system outside laboratory environments to gather data and verify conformity; does not qualify as placing on the market if conditions in Article 57 or 60 are met, as defined in Article 3(57)

The **SANDBOX PLAN** is a formal, mandatory document established as a prerequisite for participation in an AI regulatory sandbox. Defined in Article 3(54) of the relevant regulations, it functions as a specific agreement between a provider (or a prospective provider) and the competent authority overseeing the sandbox.

This document serves as the structural framework for testing activities, outlining the necessary scope, objectives, conditions, timeframe, and specific requirements for the sandbox engagement. As mandated by Article 58(1)(b), the submission of this plan is a compulsory step in the official participation procedures, ensuring that both the provider and the regulator share a clear, documented understanding of how the testing process will be conducted.

Special Categories of Personal Data refer to a sensitive classification of information that requires heightened protection under European Union law. These categories are formally defined in several key legislative frameworks, specifically Article 9(1) of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), Article 10 of Directive (EU) 2016/680, and Article 10(1) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. Due to the sensitive nature of the information involved, the processing of this data is subject to strict limitations.

However, there is an exception to the general restrictions surrounding these data categories. Providers are permitted to process this sensitive personal data in specific, exceptional circumstances, primarily for the purpose of bias detection. This processing is only lawful when accompanied by the implementation of specific safeguards, as outlined in Article 10(5) of the relevant regulations, ensuring that the integrity and confidentiality of the sensitive information are maintained throughout the process.

entity_name: EDUCATION AND VOCATIONAL TRAINING AI SYSTEM
description: AI systems used in education or vocational training for determining access, assignment to institutions, evaluating learning outcomes, assessing education levels, or monitoring prohibited student behaviour (Recital 56)
Domains or contexts for AI systems listed in Annex III that generally carry high risk unless specific exclusion conditions apply.

**OPEN-SOURCE EXCLUSION**

The "Open-Source Exclusion," as defined in Article 2(12) of the AI Act, outlines specific conditions under which the Act does not apply to artificial intelligence systems. Specifically, the regulation excludes AI systems released under free and open-source licenses from the scope of the Act's requirements. 

However, this exclusion is not absolute. The Open-Source Exclusion ceases to apply if such systems are placed on the market or put into service as high-risk AI systems. Furthermore, the exclusion does not apply if the open-source AI system falls under the scope of Article 5, which covers prohibited AI practices, or Article 50, which pertains to transparency obligations for certain AI systems. Consequently, while open-source software generally benefits from this exemption, it must still adhere to the AI Act if it crosses into these designated high-risk or prohibited categories.
The Commission must evaluate and review the Regulation by 2 August 2029 and every four years thereafter (Recital 174).

Penalties serve as a critical enforcement mechanism designed to address non-compliance with the Regulation. To ensure their legitimacy and impact, these measures must be effective, proportionate, and dissuasive. 

Under the established framework, Member States are mandated to lay down, notify, and effectively implement rules regarding these penalties—which include administrative fines—by the deadline of 2 August 2025. Furthermore, the state and ongoing effectiveness of these penalties are subject to periodic evaluation by the Commission, which is required to report its findings to the Parliament and the Council, as stipulated in Article 112(4)(b).

**MANDATORY REQUIREMENTS** constitute a comprehensive set of obligations and standards established by the AI Act to ensure the safe and ethical deployment of artificial intelligence. These measures are designed to guide providers in ensuring compliance, with a core focus on balancing strict adherence with the state of the art and the principle of proportionality, as highlighted in Recital 64.

At their core, these requirements serve as the fundamental framework for high-risk AI systems. They encompass specific technical and organizational obligations that providers of these high-risk systems are legally compelled to implement. These obligations, which the European Commission is tasked with integrating into sector-specific legislation, include rigorous protocols for risk management, the maintenance of detailed technical documentation, and clear procedures for enforcement, as outlined in Recital 50.

While the primary intent of these Mandatory Requirements is to regulate high-risk AI systems, they also serve as a benchmark for the broader AI ecosystem. Providers of AI systems that do not fall under the "high-risk" classification are encouraged to voluntarily adopt these core rules, fostering a culture of safety and transparency across the industry regardless of the system's specific classification. Ultimately, these requirements act as the essential compliance backbone for the AI Act, ensuring that systems are developed with necessary safeguards and accountability.

A "SAFETY COMPONENT" is defined as a critical element within a product that is subject to specific safety legislation, particularly in the context of high-risk AI systems as outlined in Recital 87. These are AI systems or pieces of software functioning as essential components of products, such as autonomous robots, that fall under Union harmonisation legislation based on the New Legislative Framework.

Because of their role in these systems, "SAFETY COMPONENT" entities are subject to strict regulatory requirements aimed at preventing and mitigating safety risks. Given their direct impact on the safe operation of the products they serve, these AI systems must be rigorously managed to prevent them from causing potential harm or compromising user safety.

Article 10 pertains to the establishment of regulations concerning data and data governance specifically for high-risk artificial intelligence (AI) systems. As a governing provision, this article outlines the necessary standards and requirements that must be adhered to regarding the management, quality, and oversight of data used within the development and operation of high-risk AI technologies.
The process of ensuring high-risk AI systems perform consistently for their intended purpose and comply with legal requirements, performed prior to market placement, as per Article 9(6)-(8)
Appropriate and targeted actions adopted to address identified risks, ensuring residual risk is acceptable, as described in Article 9(2)(d) and 9(5)

Article 9 outlines the mandatory requirements regarding risk management systems specifically designed for high-risk AI systems. This article establishes a regulatory framework that mandates the creation, implementation, documentation, and ongoing maintenance of a comprehensive risk management system. By requiring these processes, Article 9 ensures that organizations developing or operating high-risk AI systems have structured protocols in place to identify, assess, and mitigate potential risks throughout the lifecycle of the technology.

Article 49(5) serves as a regulatory provision within the framework governing high-risk AI systems. It specifically dictates the requirements for registration within the designated EU database for those high-risk AI systems that are related to point 2 of Annex III. Furthermore, Article 49(5) mandates that the Commission must provide appropriate guidelines to facilitate this process, ensuring these guidelines are developed only after formally consulting the European Artificial Intelligence Board.

The Risk Assessment Criteria, as outlined in Article 7(2), serve as the fundamental framework used by the Commission to evaluate and determine whether specific AI systems should be classified as high-risk and subsequently added to the Annex III list of high-risk use cases.

These criteria encompass a broad spectrum of factors designed to assess the potential impact and safety of AI technologies. They include critical operational and functional elements such as an AI system’s intended purpose, its data usage patterns, its level of autonomy, the extent of human oversight, and the potential for harm. Furthermore, the assessment integrates specific factors detailed in Article 7(2)(f-k), which delve into the nuances of risk, including the intensity of potential harm, the degree of user dependency on the system, and the vulnerability of the populations interacting with the technology. Together, these elements allow the Commission to systematically analyze AI systems and ensure appropriate regulatory categorization.
Duty imposed on providers to adhere to the Act, enforceable by penalties and fines tailored to factors such as nature, gravity, duration, and provider size

In the context of artificial intelligence, Non-Discrimination is both an essential quality and a mandatory requirement designed to ensure fairness and equity. It serves as a critical safeguard to prevent AI systems from perpetuating or amplifying historical patterns of prejudice, bias, or exclusion.

To be effective, Non-Discrimination must be deeply integrated into the development and deployment of AI technologies to protect individuals against discrimination based on protected characteristics, including age, gender, disability, race, and sexual orientation. This principle is particularly vital in high-stakes sectors such as migration, asylum, and law enforcement, where the potential for automated inequality or exclusion poses significant risks to human rights and societal well-being. By prioritizing Non-Discrimination, developers and stakeholders work to ensure that AI systems do not marginalize vulnerable populations, but instead operate in a just and equitable manner.

The Non-Discrimination Requirement mandates that AI systems deployed within the domains of migration, asylum, and border control must operate without bias to ensure the protection and guarantee of fundamental human rights. This obligation necessitates proactive measures to ensure that these technologies do not perpetuate, amplify, or solidify historical patterns of discrimination. Consequently, the Non-Discrimination Requirement strictly prohibits the unfair treatment of individuals based on protected characteristics, including age, gender, disability, race, or sexual orientation. By enforcing this standard, the requirement aims to uphold equitable treatment and safeguard the rights of all individuals interacting with these AI-driven systems.

Article 18 of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020 serves as a critical legal provision that delineates the procedural rights afforded to concerned operators during market surveillance procedures. By establishing these rights, the regulation ensures that operators are afforded due process and necessary protections when their products are subject to regulatory oversight.

Furthermore, Article 18 of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020 is extended to the domain of artificial intelligence regulation. It applies *mutatis mutandis*—meaning with the necessary changes in points of detail—to providers of general-purpose AI models. In this context, it functions as a vital safeguard, ensuring that these AI providers are granted the same fundamental procedural protections during oversight and enforcement actions as other economic operators, thereby maintaining consistency and fairness in the regulatory landscape.

**INVESTIGATIVE AND CORRECTIVE POWERS** refer to the essential authorities and mandates granted to oversight bodies, regulators, and enforcement agencies, enabling them to effectively carry out their official duties. These powers allow authorities to access necessary personal data and specific information required to perform their prescribed tasks and functions.

In the context of law enforcement and the justice domain, these powers are critical, as they provide the legal framework for authorities to gather the evidence and data needed to uphold the law and administer justice. Similarly, within regulatory frameworks, these investigative and corrective powers are specifically granted to market surveillance authorities, ensuring they have the requisite access to information to monitor compliance, identify irregularities, and maintain market integrity. Ultimately, these powers function as a vital toolset that enables diverse public authorities to fulfill their responsibilities by ensuring they have the appropriate level of access to data while performing their necessary operations.

entity_name: ADMINISTRATION OF JUSTICE AND DEMOCRATIC PROCESSES
description: The "Administration of Justice and Democratic Processes" encompasses high-risk artificial intelligence use case domains that are integral to the functioning of critical public sectors. Specifically, this area pertains to AI systems utilized to assist judicial authorities in performing legal tasks, as well as systems that have the potential to influence the outcomes of elections or referenda. These applications are formally categorized as high-risk within the framework of AI regulations, such as those detailed in Annex III(8), reflecting the profound societal impact these technologies exert on governance and the rule of law.

The Competent Authorities for Financial Services are the regulatory and supervisory bodies specifically mandated to oversee and enforce financial services law within the European Union. These authorities are formally defined under Regulation (EU) No 575/2013, as well as Directives 2008/48/EC, 2009/138/EC, and 2013/36/EU.

Beyond their established duties in the financial sector, these Competent Authorities for Financial Services have been designated to supervise the implementation and enforcement of the AI Act specifically regarding financial institutions. Consequently, they hold the dual responsibility of maintaining regulatory standards in financial services while ensuring that financial institutions comply with the specific AI obligations mandated by European Union regulations.

Union AI Testing Support Structures are specialized entities designated by the European Commission. Their primary mandate is to perform essential testing tasks in accordance with Regulation (EU) 2019/1020. Beyond their testing responsibilities, these structures are tasked with providing independent, expert technical and scientific advice. Furthermore, these structures are established to support the enforcement of AI regulations and to reinforce the overall capacities of Member States concerning AI systems.

**GENERAL-PURPOSE AI MODELS** are subject to specific monitoring activities conducted by the AI Office. These models may also be subject to qualified alerts, regarding which the Board is tasked with providing opinions to the Commission, in accordance with Article 66(n).

Mandatory requirements for high-risk AI systems comprise essential technical and organizational standards designed to ensure that these systems do not pose unacceptable risks. As outlined in Recital 46, these requirements serve the dual purpose of preventing harm in a general sense and specifically safeguarding Union public interests from the potential threats posed by high-risk AI deployment.

Requirement for at least two natural persons to verify and confirm identification resulting from certain high-risk AI systems (Annex III point 1(a)), with specific exemptions for law enforcement/migration contexts

**ARTICLE 79(1)**

ARTICLE 79(1) serves as a foundational provision for establishing the regulatory framework surrounding artificial intelligence. Its primary purpose is to define the concept of "risk" specifically within the context of AI systems. This definition is critical for identifying and categorizing potential hazards that may arise during the operation of AI technologies.

Furthermore, ARTICLE 79(1) delineates the specific thresholds and parameters that constitute a "risk" requiring formal attention or intervention. This includes establishing the context necessary for identifying situations where a high-risk AI system might pose a danger. Consequently, the article plays a pivotal role in the reporting process, as it clearly outlines the nature of the risks associated with high-risk AI systems that mandate notification by importers to the relevant regulatory authorities. By standardizing the definition and understanding of these risks, ARTICLE 79(1) ensures a consistent approach to the monitoring and oversight of AI systems.

The Simplified Technical Documentation Form is a specific template established by the Commission for use by small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs). This form is designed to facilitate the provision of technical documentation in a simplified format, as permitted under Article 11(1). By utilizing this standardized template, SMEs can streamline the process of submitting the necessary technical information required for regulatory compliance.

Ireland is a Member State of the European Union that maintains a distinct legal position regarding certain aspects of judicial and police cooperation in criminal matters. Due to specific opt-out provisions outlined in Article 6a of Protocol No. 21, Ireland is not bound by particular regulations in this domain. This unique status impacts the extent of its obligations under the AI Act, specifically concerning provisions related to police and judicial cooperation within the European Union.

Deployers must request ex-ante authorisation (or no later than 48 hours) from a judicial or administrative authority for the use of post-remote biometric identification systems, except in specific cases of initial identification.
Deployers use information provided under Article 13 to comply with their obligation to carry out a DPIA under GDPR or Directive (EU) 2016/680.
Refers to the section of the AI Act containing requirements that high-risk AI systems must meet

Annex VIII serves as a critical regulatory component within the framework governing artificial intelligence, specifically outlining the mandatory information requirements for the registration of high-risk AI systems in the EU database. This annex ensures compliance and transparency by detailing the specific data that must be submitted under Article 49 of the relevant regulations. Furthermore, Annex VIII is structured into specific sections—designated as sections A, B, and C—which comprehensively list the precise data points that are required to be entered into the EU database to complete the registration process.
Third-country providers must appoint an authorised representative via written mandate and empower them to perform tasks like verifying conformity as per Article 22
Providers must provide documentation and logs upon a reasoned request by a competent authority as per Article 21
The standing sub-group for market surveillance, acting as the administrative cooperation group (ADCO) for the Regulation per Regulation (EU) 2019/1020

STANDARDISED TEMPLATES are essential documentation aids designed to streamline regulatory adherence. These documents are provided by the Commission with the specific objective of addressing the unique needs of small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs), thereby facilitating their compliance efforts. Furthermore, these templates are furnished by the AI Office, in accordance with specifications set forth by the Board, to assist organizations in meeting the compliance requirements mandated under Article 62(3)(a). Collectively, they serve as a critical resource for simplifying complex regulatory obligations.

Regulation (EU) 2016/679, widely known as the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), serves as a fundamental framework for data protection and privacy within the European Union. Its provisions impose strict limitations on the processing of personal data, ensuring that such activities are conducted lawfully, fairly, and transparently.

In addition to these general processing limits, Regulation (EU) 2016/679 establishes mandatory compliance requirements for specific technological applications. Notably, it acts as a critical regulatory benchmark for deployers of emotion recognition and biometric categorization systems. Organizations implementing these advanced technologies must adhere to the stringent standards set forth by the GDPR to protect individual rights and freedoms, particularly given the sensitive nature of biometric and emotional data.
Service providers subject to obligations under Article 16 of Regulation (EU) 2022/2065 regarding illegal content notices, which remains independent of transparency labeling obligations (Recital 136)
Article concerning changes to notifications, including cessation of activities, suspension, and withdrawal of designation

Regarding the "NOTIFICATION OF NATIONAL RULES," Member States are required to notify the European Commission of any national regulations they adopt concerning the authorization and use of biometric identification systems. This mandate specifically pertains to remote biometric identification systems. Such notifications must be submitted to the Commission no later than 30 days following the formal adoption of these rules.

European Standardisation Organisations (ESOs) are crucial entities operating within the framework of Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012. These organisations have been tasked by the European Commission with the critical responsibility of drafting harmonised standards specifically for high-risk AI systems and general-purpose AI models. Furthermore, European Standardisation Organisations serve as essential forums for engagement, as notified bodies are required to participate in or be represented within these groups to ensure they remain fully aware of and aligned with all relevant standards.

The formal submission by a conformity assessment body to a notifying authority to become a notified body, including proof of competence, accreditation, and descriptions of activities, as per Article 29

The **AUTHORISATION OBLIGATION** refers to a regulatory mandate placed on law enforcement agencies regarding the use of remote biometric identification. Under this obligation, law enforcement is generally required to secure authorization—either *ex ante* (prior to the action) or *post-hoc* (within 48 hours after the action)—from a relevant judicial or administrative authority before proceeding with remote biometric identification.

However, there is a specific exception to this mandate. The requirement to obtain such authorization does not apply when law enforcement is identifying a potential suspect based on verifiable facts. In these specific instances, the procedural hurdle of seeking judicial or administrative approval is waived to facilitate the identification process.

Article 67 serves as the foundational provision that establishes the Advisory Forum. This article outlines the formal framework for the Forum, explicitly detailing its membership structure, core duties, term of office, and the operational rules that govern its conduct. 

In addition to defining its organizational structure, Article 67 mandates the Advisory Forum’s role in specific regulatory processes, stipulating that the Forum must be consulted regarding the development or implementation of common specifications. Furthermore, Article 67 provides the necessary legal reference for the Advisory Forum’s engagement with other governing bodies, specifically authorizing its representatives to attend Management Board meetings.

IDENTITY CHECK refers to the authorized procedural process of identifying individuals who are either unable or unwilling to provide proof of identity. This measure is preserved under both Union and national law, ensuring that specific designated authorities retain the power to verify the identities of such persons.

As a functional capability, IDENTITY CHECK is designated for use by key state actors, including law enforcement, border control, immigration, and asylum authorities. By maintaining this authority, these bodies are equipped to carry out essential identification duties even when standard cooperation from the individual is not forthcoming. This procedural framework ensures that the ability to identify persons is upheld and regulated, providing a clear legal basis for these authorities to operate within established guidelines.

CHAPTER V of the AI Act establishes critical obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models. This section specifically addresses the requirements related to transparency and systemic risk, as outlined in its second and third sections. 

Enforcement of these provisions falls under the jurisdiction of the European Commission, as stipulated by Article 88. According to Article 113 of the regulation, CHAPTER V is set to become applicable on 2 August 2025.

entity_name: OFFICIAL JOURNAL OF THE EUROPEAN UNION
description: The Official Journal of the European Union serves as the official publication medium for references to harmonised standards and common specifications. Its role is pivotal in the regulatory framework of the European Union, as the publication of these references within the Journal is the required mechanism to trigger specific legal effects. Notably, this publication is the formal step necessary to establish the presumption of conformity for products or services adhering to these standards.
The specific sections of the AI Act setting out obligations for general-purpose AI models.
The specific section of the AI Act setting out requirements for high-risk AI systems.
A document issued by a notified body attesting to a system's conformity; its validity status is subject to specific conditions when the issuing body's designation is changed.

The **DESIGNATION MANAGEMENT OBLIGATION** refers to the comprehensive set of procedural duties imposed on a notifying authority following a change in designation, such as a restriction, suspension, or withdrawal of a notified body. This process ensures regulatory oversight, transparency, and continuity for affected providers and certificates.

Upon a designation change, the notifying authority is mandated to maintain comprehensive files and assess the impact of the change on any certificates previously issued by the notified body. The authority must report its findings and actions to the Commission within three months of the designation change. Furthermore, the authority is required to inform all relevant authorities of the situation and must notify the affected providers within 10 days of any suspension or restriction.

When certificates are involved, they may remain valid under specific conditions—typically requiring confirmation from the notifying authority—or they may be revoked if deemed to have been unduly issued. The notifying authority must provide clear, mandatory instructions to the notified body regarding these certificates. If a provider needs to transition to a new notified body, they are granted a period of up to three months to secure such confirmation. In certain circumstances, certificate validity may be extended for a total duration of up to 12 months.

In summary, the **DESIGNATION MANAGEMENT OBLIGATION** serves as a structured framework requiring notifying authorities to manage administrative records, conduct impact assessments, communicate effectively with both the Commission and affected providers, and oversee the status and validity of certificates throughout the transition period.

**ADMINISTRATION OF JUSTICE AI SYSTEM**

The "Administration of Justice AI System" refers to advanced artificial intelligence technologies designed specifically for use by, or on behalf of, judicial authorities. The primary purpose of these systems is to support the judiciary in the complex tasks inherent in legal proceedings. Specifically, these AI tools are intended to assist in the comprehensive processes of researching, interpreting, and analyzing both facts and the law, as well as applying the relevant legal frameworks to those specific facts.

"VULNERABLE GROUPS" refers to specific populations that require special attention, heightened protection, and nuanced consideration during the implementation of AI systems and related regulatory processes. This category primarily encompasses individuals under the age of 18, as well as persons with disabilities or other conditions that might render them particularly susceptible to adverse impacts.

The identification of these groups is central to robust risk management processes. Because these individuals may be disproportionately affected by high-risk AI systems, their specific needs and vulnerabilities must be carefully assessed to mitigate potential harm. Furthermore, the inclusion of "VULNERABLE GROUPS" is critical when applying transparency obligations, as their unique characteristics necessitate tailored communication and engagement strategies to ensure they are adequately protected. Additionally, these groups are recognized as subjects of real-world testing who require specific, protective oversight to ensure their safety and well-being throughout the development and deployment lifecycle of technology.

The Civil Protection Authority is a governmental body tasked with the oversight and management of civil protection measures. In performing its duties, the Civil Protection Authority possesses the specific mandate to deploy high-risk AI systems without obtaining prior authorization from market surveillance authorities. This ability to bypass standard authorization procedures is strictly limited to exceptional, urgent, or justified situations, ensuring that the Authority can effectively respond to critical events without being hindered by regulatory delays.

Notified Bodies are professional third-party certifiers that play a critical role in the regulatory framework established by the AI Act. Specifically, these entities serve as conformity assessment bodies tasked with evaluating high-risk AI systems to ensure they meet established safety and performance standards. In this capacity, Notified Bodies are subject to a strict set of obligations and regulatory requirements, ensuring that their oversight procedures remain rigorous, transparent, and compliant with the mandates set forth by the AI Act.
Requirement for providers to mark AI-generated outputs in a machine-readable format to ensure they are detectable as artificially generated

"UNACCEPTABLE RISK" is the classification level assigned to specific artificial intelligence practices that are prohibited under regulatory frameworks, as detailed in Article 5 of the relevant legislation. This category represents the highest level of risk associated with AI applications. The prohibition of practices falling under this classification is further elaborated in Recitals 29 and 30, which provide context regarding the nature of these prohibited activities. According to Recital 179, the enforcement of these prohibitions must be applied starting from 2 February 2025.

**ARTICLE 6(3)** is a regulatory provision that outlines the framework for classifying AI systems, specifically focusing on the threshold between systems deemed "high-risk" and those that are not. 

This article plays a crucial role in the classification process by establishing the conditions under which an AI system is designated as high-risk, often by referencing criteria found in Annex III. Conversely, it serves as the definitive reference point for determining whether an AI system falls outside the high-risk category.

When providers conclude that an AI system does not meet the high-risk criteria, **ARTICLE 6(3)** mandates that they must provide formal justification for this assessment during the mandatory registration process. Even though these systems are not classified as high-risk, the provision clarifies that they may still be subject to specific registration requirements, ensuring regulatory oversight for systems that do not trigger the stricter high-risk obligations. In essence, **ARTICLE 6(3)** acts as a dual-purpose mechanism, functioning both as a filter for identifying high-risk AI and as a compliance guideline for systems that providers determine are not high-risk but which must still be formally registered.
The process triggered when objections are raised against an authorization, requiring the Commission to consult with the relevant Member State and affected operators.
The process by which law enforcement or civil protection authorities may seek permission to use a high-risk AI system in emergencies; if refused, use must cease and results be discarded.

The "REQUIREMENTS OF SECTION 2" constitute the essential criteria that high-risk AI systems must satisfy to achieve and maintain their regulatory compliance and certification status under the AI Act. Specifically, these mandates, which are referenced in Article 47(2), encompass a comprehensive framework of both technical and organizational requirements. By adhering to these standards, AI systems ensure they are operating within the established legal and safety parameters required by the legislation.

An authorization mechanism allowing law enforcement or civil protection authorities to deploy high-risk AI systems in urgent, life-threatening situations without prior authorization, subject to post-hoc request.
An authorization by a market surveillance authority allowing the placing on the market of a high-risk AI system without completing standard conformity assessment procedures for exceptional reasons.
Requirement for providers and deployers to enable detection and disclosure that outputs are artificially generated or manipulated
A framework provided by Regulation (EU) 2022/2065 for managing risks associated with very large online platforms and search engines

entity_name: AI SYSTEM FOR ELECTION OR REFERENDUM INFLUENCE
description: An AI system intended to be used to influence the outcome of an election or referendum or voting behaviour of natural persons. Classified as high-risk (Recital 62).

entity_name: AI SYSTEM FOR ADMINISTRATION OF JUSTICE
description: An AI system intended to be used by a judicial authority or on its behalf to assist in researching and interpreting facts and the law, or applying the law to a concrete set of facts. Considered high-risk per Recital 61.

Civil society organisations are formally recognised as key stakeholders within the regulatory framework governing the development of codes of practice and conduct. They are integrated into the legislative process to ensure diverse perspectives are represented during the drafting phases. Specifically, as outlined in Article 56(3), civil society organisations are invited to support and contribute to the process of drawing up codes of practice. Furthermore, in accordance with Article 95(3), they are identified as interested stakeholders who may be actively involved in the creation of codes of conduct. Their engagement is further highlighted by Recital 116, which notes that the AI Office is tasked with consulting these stakeholders during the drafting of the codes of practice. Through these provisions, civil society organisations play an essential role in providing input, ensuring transparency, and fostering inclusivity in the development of regulatory standards.

**CYBERSECURITY PROTECTION**

CYBERSECURITY PROTECTION represents a mandatory obligation to ensure an adequate and robust level of security for AI models and their supporting physical infrastructure. As stipulated by Article 55(1)(d), this duty requires that rigorous security measures be implemented to safeguard the integrity and safety of the technology.

This protection extends to the entire operational ecosystem, encompassing critical components such as the models themselves, their specific weights, underlying algorithms, the servers hosting them, and the associated data sets. As outlined in Recital 115, the primary objective of this requirement is to proactively prevent malicious use, unauthorized access, or external attacks that could compromise the system. By securing both the digital assets and the physical infrastructure, CYBERSECURITY PROTECTION serves as a fundamental control to mitigate risks and maintain the integrity of the AI model throughout its lifecycle.

Article 56 functions as a critical regulatory provision specifically addressing the codes of practice for general-purpose AI models. It defines the meaning and scope of these codes, establishing the essential framework and outlining the development process required for their implementation. By setting these standards, Article 56 provides the necessary guidance and structure for the governance of general-purpose AI technologies.

Prohibited AI practices, as outlined under Article 5, encompass a specific set of actions that are forbidden due to the risks they pose to individuals and society. These regulations strictly prohibit the deployment or use of AI systems that utilize manipulative techniques designed to distort human behavior or exploit the specific vulnerabilities of particular groups or individuals. Such practices are banned because they threaten fundamental rights, can cause significant harm, and directly contradict the core values of the Union. By banning these specific applications, the framework seeks to ensure that AI technologies are developed and used in a manner that protects human autonomy and safety.

**ANNEX XI** is a specific and essential component of the EU AI Act. It serves as the formal section that outlines the comprehensive requirements for the technical documentation of general-purpose AI models. By detailing the specific content that must be included within these documents, ANNEX XI ensures transparency and regulatory compliance for developers working with these advanced systems.
Duty to draw up and keep up-to-date technical documentation of the model (including training/testing process and results) per Article 53(1)(a).

**ANNEX XIII** serves as a regulatory component that explicitly outlines the criteria used to determine whether a general-purpose AI (GPAI) model should be designated as presenting systemic risks. This annex provides the specific framework utilized by the European Commission for assessing and designating such models, directly supporting the requirements established in Article 51 of the underlying AI legislation.

**Article 51** serves as the regulatory framework establishing the classification and specific criteria used to identify general-purpose AI (GPAI) models that pose systemic risks. It defines the conditions under which a GPAI model is categorized as having systemic risk, a designation that includes criteria for high-impact capabilities and specific compute thresholds. Furthermore, this article is cross-referenced in Article 90, highlighting its pivotal role in the governance structure for high-impact AI systems. In essence, Article 51 provides the foundational legal standards for determining which general-purpose AI models are subject to systemic risk oversight and regulation.
An AI system that generates or manipulates image, audio or video content constituting a deep fake

The Risk-Based Approach is a regulatory framework and methodology designed to manage the development and deployment of artificial intelligence (AI) systems. It functions by tailoring the intensity and scope of binding rules and regulatory requirements according to the specific level of risk generated by an AI system. 

Under this framework, the approach is categorized into three primary components: the prohibition of practices deemed unacceptable, the imposition of strict requirements for systems classified as high-risk, and the enforcement of transparency obligations for AI applications. By aligning the stringency of oversight with the potential harm an AI system may cause, the Risk-Based Approach ensures that regulatory interventions are proportionate, focused, and effective in mitigating safety and ethical concerns.
Duty to register real-world testing in the EU database or according to Art 71(4), with exceptions for specific Annex III high-risk systems
Acts adopted by the Commission to specify detailed arrangements for the establishment, operation, and supervision of AI regulatory sandboxes, ensuring consistency across the Union (Article 58(1)).

entity_name: ANNUAL REPORT ON AI REGULATORY SANDBOXES
description: A mandatory report submitted by national competent authorities to the AI Office and the Board regarding the progress, results, best practices, and incidents within the sandboxes (Recital 16).
Implementing acts established by the Commission to mandate obligations from Articles 53 and 55 if a code of practice is not finalized or is deemed inadequate by 2 August 2025

ACADEMIA refers to academic institutions and stakeholders invited to participate in the development of regulatory frameworks. Specifically, ACADEMIA is recognized as an interested party capable of supporting the process of drafting both codes of practice and codes of conduct. In the context of regulatory compliance, academic institutions are invited to provide their expertise to assist in drawing up codes of practice, as outlined in Article 56(3).
